title: The Grief That Comes with Healing
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: The grief that comes with healing complex trauma is a profound loss. Learn how to navigate this difficult but necessary part of the journey back to yourself.
imageDescription: A delicate porcelain bowl, shattered and then repaired with shimmering gold, sits on a rustic wooden table. Warm, gentle morning light streams in from a nearby window, illuminating the golden cracks and casting a soft glow on the scene. The image evokes a sense of beauty in imperfection and the healing power of repair.
htmlContent:
Healing is not an arrival. It is a constant act of grieving for the person you had to be to survive.

The Unacknowledged Loss of Self

We talk about healing as if it is a destination, a clean shore we wash up on after a long and harrowing work through the storm of the past. But for those of us who have navigated the treacherous waters of complex trauma, the reality is far more textured, far more layered. The work of healing is often, at its core, a work of grieving. It is the slow, painful, and significantly necessary process of mourning the childhood we never had, the safety we never felt, and the self that was lost in the chaos of survival. We are not just grieving the things that happened; we are grieving the things that didn’t happen... the secure attachment, the spontaneous joy, the unwavering belief in our own worth. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen countless individuals grapple with this real sense of loss, a grief so deep and pervasive it can feel like a fundamental part of their being.

It turns out to be not a grief that can be reasoned with or rationalized away. It lives in the body, in the tight knot in the stomach, the ache in the chest, the constant, low-level hum of anxiety. It is the body’s memory of a world that was not safe, a world where vigilance was the only currency that mattered. We must, as the pioneering work of Stephen Porges has shown us, learn the language of our own nervous system, to understand its signals not as pathologies to be fixed but as communications to be heard. It requires that we bypass the thinking mind and drop into the raw data of sensation, for it is here, in the pre-verbal, pre-cognitive space of the body, that the real story of the past is held and where the potential for a new future can be found.

The Architecture of Survival

The person you became to survive was a masterpiece of adaptation. Every defense mechanism, every emotional wall, every pattern of relating was a brilliant, life-saving strategy in the face of overwhelming circumstances. To begin to heal is to begin to dismantle this architecture of survival, and that dismantling is, itself, a form of loss. We grieve the loss of the identity we constructed, even when that identity was a cage. A client once described this as feeling like they were standing in the rubble of their own life, with no blueprint for what to build next. It is a disorienting and terrifying place to be, a liminal space between the no longer and the not yet.

The Myth of Moving On

Our culture is obsessed with the idea of “moving on,” of leaving the past behind and striding confidently into the future. But for those who carry the weight of complex trauma, this is a cruel and impossible demand. There is no moving on from something that is still living inside of you. The grief that comes with healing is not a sign that you are stuck or broken; it is a sign that you are finally, bravely, turning toward the truth of what you have endured. It is proof of your resilience, to your capacity to feel, and to your unwavering commitment to your own liberation.

A resource specifically for abuse survivors that many find supportive is The Courage to Heal (paid link).

Let that land for a moment.

The Field of the Heart

As we allow ourselves to grieve, the territory of our hearts begins to change. The barren, desolate places begin to soften. The frozen rivers of emotion begin to thaw. It turns out to be the love that Kalesh speaks of in his work, a love that is not a sentiment but a state of being, a fundamental recognition of our own inherent worth. It is a love that can hold all of our broken pieces, all of our contradictions, all of our messy, beautiful humanity. It is a love that says, “You are welcome here, exactly as you are.”

It turns out to be not a passive process. It is an active and ongoing practice of turning toward ourselves with kindness and curiosity. It is the practice of meeting our own pain with the same compassion we would offer to a dear friend. It is the practice of forgiving ourselves for the ways we had to survive, for the choices we made when we had no other options. And as we do this, as we cultivate this inner terrain of love and acceptance, we begin to find that the grief is not the end of the story. It is the beginning of a new one, a story of a heart that has been broken open, a story of a self that is being reborn. A story that is explored in more detail in Kalesh's teachings on consciousness.

The Eloquence of Tears

There will be tears. There will be moments when the grief feels like a tidal wave, threatening to pull you under. And in those moments, the most radical act of self-love is to let the tears come. Each tear is a prayer, a release, a letting go of a burden you have carried for far too long. Each sob is a song of liberation, a reclaiming of the parts of yourself that have been silenced and shamed. What we find here is not a sign of weakness; it is a sign of real strength, of a courage that is willing to feel it all.

A memoir that many C-PTSD survivors deeply connect with is What My Bones Know (paid link).

We have been taught to fear our own emotions, to see them as problems to be solved or enemies to be vanquished. But what if our emotions are not the enemy? What if they are simply energy in motion, messengers from the deep, asking for our attention, our presence, our compassionate witness? What if the grief is not something to be overcome, but something to be honored, to be held, to be allowed to move through us in its own time and in its own way? It is a path from the head to the heart, from the story to the sensation, from the past to the present moment. And it is a path that is worth taking, for on the other side of the grief lies a love that is vaster and more unconditional than anything we could have ever imagined. It is a work of understanding our own Four F Responses.

The Sacred Work of Integration

Ultimately, the grief that comes with healing is not about what we have lost, but about what we are reclaiming. We are reclaiming our right to feel, our right to be messy and imperfect, our right to take up space in the world. We are reclaiming our bodies, our voices, our stories. It turns out to be the sacred work of integration, of bringing all the disparate pieces of ourselves back into a cohesive and coherent whole. It is the work of a lifetime, a road without a final destination, a path that unfolds with each step we take.

The real question is not whether this applies to you. You already know it does. The real question is what you are going to do with that knowledge now that you cannot un-know it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does healing have to involve so much grief?

Grief is the natural and necessary response to loss, and healing from complex trauma involves acknowledging and processing a multitude of losses. We grieve the loss of a safe and nurturing childhood, the loss of our sense of self, and the loss of the life we might have had if the trauma had not occurred. It is the emotional expression of coming to terms with reality, and it is through this process that we can begin to integrate the past and create a new future.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link).

I know, I know.

What if I feel like the grief will never end?

It is a common and understandable fear that the grief will be overwhelming and endless. However, the process of grieving is not a linear one, and it does not last forever. The intensity of the grief will lessen over time, and it will be interspersed with periods of joy, connection, and gratitude. The goal is not to eliminate grief, but to expand our capacity to hold it alongside all the other emotions of a full life.

How can I support myself through the grieving process?

Supporting yourself through the grieving process involves a combination of self-compassion, somatic awareness, and connection with others. Practices like mindfulness, gentle movement, and spending time in nature can help to regulate the nervous system and create a sense of safety in the body. Connecting with a therapist, a support group, or trusted friends can provide the relational support that is so essential for healing. Remember that you are not broken, and you do not need to be fixed. You are simply a human being who has endured a great deal and is now finding your way home.

There is a tenderness that is earned through the courageous act of turning toward our own pain. It is a tenderness that is forged in the fires of grief, a tenderness that is born of a heart that has been broken open and made more spacious, more compassionate, more alive. It is the discovery that even in our most broken places, we are still and always whole.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

Are you willing to be uncomfortable long enough for something to shift?
